network.overall_strategy: This network treats glutamate as a modular, convergent prebiotic product centered on the experimentally supported hub α-ketoglutarate rather than as a one-pot outcome. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon through Fe0/FeS/greigite/magnetite-promoted CO2 fixation to formate and pyruvate, while mild hydrothermal protometabolism generates α-ketoglutarate from pyruvate plus glyoxylate under Fe2+ conditions. Surface environments contribute HCN-based chemistry, atmospheric discharge products, acrolein-based Strecker chemistry, HCN oligomer pools, and cyanosulfidic nitrile precursors under UV and mineral control. Biochemical/prebiotic stages then convert α-ketoglutarate to glutamate by multiple experimentally demonstrated routes: transamination, NADH-mediated reductive amination, H2/Ni-catalyzed reductive amination, FeS/H2S amination, pyrite-photocatalyzed amination, and protometabolic Fe0/hydroxylamine-assisted networks. The design explicitly follows literature showing that glutamate accumulation is favored by stepwise compartmentalization and is disfavored in one-pot mixed systems.

reactions.name: Fe0-driven hydrothermal reduction of CO2 to formate

conditions.catalyst: elemental iron with iron-sulfur mineral surfaces
conditions.other: mild hydrothermal/serpentinization-like setting; alkaline to near-neutral reducing fluids

reactions.mechanism: Surface-mediated electron transfer from Fe0 and H2 reduces CO2 to C1 organics, with formate as a major early product.
reactions.reasoning: Directly follows native-iron reduction of CO2 to formate under prebiotic aqueous conditions and iron-sulfur vent analog chemistry; formate is a documented product in Fe0-promoted networks and on FeS/greigite surfaces (Varma et al., 2018; Preiner et al., 2020; Roldan et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Fe0-driven hydrothermal reduction of CO2 to pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: elemental iron with iron oxide surfaces
conditions.other: native iron-promoted carbon fixation; mild hydrothermal regime rather than high-temperature black smoker conditions

reactions.mechanism: Progressive reduction and C-C bond-forming chemistry in Fe0-rich aqueous systems produces pyruvate among acetyl-CoA-like fixation products.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate formation from CO2 in native iron systems is explicitly reported and is central to protometabolic entry into the TCA-like network; restricting to mild hydrothermal conditions avoids conflict with glutamate instability at high temperature (Varma et al., 2018; Amend & Shock, 1998; Dick, 2021).

reactions.name: Atmospheric spark-discharge generation of HCN and acrolein precursor pool

conditions.catalyst: mineral surfaces concentrate and preserve atmospheric products after deposition
conditions.other: spark discharge or lightning in reduced volcanic atmosphere followed by rainout into ponds

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge in reduced gas mixtures generates nitriles and unsaturated carbonyls; deposited products are concentrated on mineral-rich surface waters.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is intentionally encoded as atmospheric product delivery rather than a mechanistic single-flask synthesis. HCN and glutamate-related carbonyl precursors are part of Miller-Urey-type reduced-carbon discharge chemistry, and acrolein is specifically identified as the key Strecker precursor for glutamate in reanalysis of Miller samples (Miller, 1955; Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011; Van Trump & Miller, 1972).

reactions.name: UV photolysis generation of HCN-rich surface inventory

conditions.other: strong UV irradiation under anoxic atmosphere with H2S-bearing reduced gases

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical processing of reduced atmospheric gases generates nitriles including HCN that are subsequently trapped in surface reservoirs.
reactions.reasoning: UV photolysis of reduced CH4/C2H6/NH3/H2S atmospheres produces amino acids and relevant nitrile feedstocks; this is a literature-supported source of atmospheric organics feeding surface chemistry (Sagan & Khare, 1971; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: HCN concentration and oligomer hydrolysis to glyoxylate-rich product pool

conditions.catalyst: adsorptive concentration on clay and evaporative/eutectic enrichment
conditions.other: requires concentrated HCN, approximately >=0.01 M, enabled by freezing or evaporation; hydrolysis of heterogeneous oligomer mixtures

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to nitrile-rich polymers whose subsequent hydrolysis yields mixed carbonyl/carboxylate products including glyoxylate-related species.
reactions.reasoning: This is included conservatively as a low-selectivity source of glyoxylate-compatible feedstock rather than a clean reaction. The literature supports HCN oligomerization under concentrated conditions and hydrolytic release of diverse products; concentration is the key bottleneck (Ferris et al., 1978; Sanchez et al., 1966; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Fe2+-promoted protometabolic formation of α-ketoglutarate from pyruvate and glyoxylate

conditions.catalyst: dissolved Fe2+ promoted network with mineral buffering surfaces
conditions.other: 70C, approximately 3 h, mild hydrothermal mixing-zone conditions

reactions.mechanism: Fe2+-assisted aldol-like carbon-carbon bond formation and redox rearrangements convert pyruvate and glyoxylate into TCA-like intermediates including α-ketoglutarate.
reactions.reasoning: This is one of the strongest experimentally grounded glutamate precursor steps: Fe2+ promotes formation of 9 of 11 TCA intermediates including α-ketoglutarate from pyruvate and glyoxylate at 70C. Mineral agents are included to satisfy environmental geochemistry while keeping the dissolved-iron result central (Muchowska et al., 2019; Varma et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Metal-free warm aqueous formation of α-ketoglutarate from pyruvate and glyoxylate

conditions.other: pH 4.5, warm aqueous protometabolic pool, metal-free aldol/Cannizzaro-type chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate and glyoxylate undergo metal-free carbon-carbon bond formation and redox redistribution to generate α-ketoglutarate analogs and related TCA intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: A second independent route to the same hub is required by the literature: α-ketoglutarate forms without metals from pyruvate plus glyoxylate at pH 4.5 and 70C, supporting environmental redundancy (Stubbs et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Transamination of α-ketoglutarate with glycine to glutamate

conditions.other: pH 5, 4 h, alum-catalyzed transamination

reactions.mechanism: Nonenzymatic amino-group transfer from glycine to α-ketoglutarate yields glutamate under mildly acidic warm conditions.
reactions.reasoning: This is the highest-yield route in the supplied literature, giving about 44% glutamate, and directly supports α-ketoglutarate as the decisive metabolic hub (Stubbs et al., 2020).

reactions.name: NADH-mediated reductive amination of α-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: pH > 7

reactions.mechanism: Imine formation between α-ketoglutarate and ammonia is followed by hydride transfer from NADH to give glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: This route is explicitly demonstrated with 33% yield under room-temperature alkaline conditions and is retained despite incomplete upstream NADH synthesis because the supplied literature treats it as a plausible prebiotic cofactor-mediated route (Nogal et al., 2024).

reactions.name: H2-driven Ni-catalyzed reductive amination of α-ketoglutarate

conditions.catalyst: nickel catalyst or meteoritic Fe/Ni surface
conditions.other: pH 8; individual step works, but one-pot integrated network does not accumulate glutamate

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia condenses with α-ketoglutarate and H2 provides reducing equivalents over Ni surface to afford glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: This closely follows the reported H2-driven reductive amination conditions and explicitly respects the literature caveat that the isolated step succeeds whereas the full one-pot network fails to produce detectable glutamate (Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: FeS/H2S-assisted reductive amination of α-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: 100C hydrothermal simulation; mild vent rather than extreme black smoker conditions

reactions.mechanism: Iron-sulfur minerals facilitate imine formation and reduction of α-keto acids by sulfur-rich redox chemistry, yielding the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: FeS/H2S-driven reductive amination of α-keto acids at 100C is experimentally demonstrated and is directly applicable to α-ketoglutarate to glutamate, though glutamate stability at higher temperature remains a concern (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Pérez-Villa et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Pyrite-photocatalyzed amination of α-ketoglutarate to glutamate

conditions.other: surface photochemistry under strong UV; natural pyrite crystals provide chiral surface effects

reactions.mechanism: Photoexcited pyrite surface promotes reductive amination of α-keto acids by surface electron transfer under UV irradiation.
reactions.reasoning: The recent photocatalytic synthesis on natural pyrite specifically supports reductive amination of α-keto acids to amino acids including glutamate under UV irradiation; this also connects to chiral surface amplification discussions (Nature Communications, 2024).

reactions.name: Fe0/hydroxylamine protometabolic conversion to glutamate in TCA-like network

conditions.catalyst: Fe0 with dissolved Fe2+ generated in situ
conditions.other: 70C, complex protometabolic network; glutamate detected among products rather than as a selective single-step output

reactions.mechanism: A network of Fe-driven carbon-carbon bond formation, redox transformations, and nitrogen incorporation from hydroxylamine yields glutamate within a broader TCA-like reaction system.
reactions.reasoning: Included exactly as a network-level demonstrated route rather than a selective transformation. Muchowska et al. reported glutamate among products when hydroxylamine and Fe0 were added to the pyruvate/glyoxylate TCA-like system (Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Acrolein-based Strecker formation of glutamate aminonitrile

conditions.catalyst: surface concentration and nitrile-promoting mineral phases
conditions.other: Michael addition of HCN to acrolein followed by Strecker-type aminonitrile formation in aqueous surface pools

reactions.mechanism: Acrolein undergoes conjugate addition with HCN to generate a C4 nitrile-aldehyde precursor, which then reacts with NH3/HCN to form the glutamate aminonitrile pool.
reactions.reasoning: The acrolein route is explicitly identified in the literature as the key classical Strecker route to glutamate, supported by Miller sample reanalysis and equilibrium studies at high dilution. The representation is kept centered on the aminonitrile intermediate, not compressed directly to glutamate (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Miller & Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glutamate aminonitrile to glutamate

conditions.other: progressive nitrile hydrolysis during warm aqueous cycling

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydrolysis converts the aminonitrile intermediate into the corresponding dicarboxylic amino acid, glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Aminonitrile hydrolysis is the accepted completion step of the classical Strecker pathway to amino acids; for glutamate this route is specifically tied to acrolein-derived intermediates in the cited work (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization-hydrolysis as a low-yield background source of glutamate

conditions.other: requires concentrated HCN, approximately 0.1 M, pH about 9.2, followed by hydrolysis; very low selectivity

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes into complex nitrile polymers that upon hydrolysis release a broad product distribution including minor glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: This route is intentionally labeled as diffuse and low-selectivity, not a flagship pathway. It is nevertheless experimentally demonstrated that hydrolyzed HCN oligomer mixtures can release glutamic acid (Ferris et al., 1978; Moser & Matthews, 1968; Sanchez et al., 1966).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic production of acrylonitrile precursor

conditions.other: HCN/H2S/UV photoredox network in shallow surface waters

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven sulfur-assisted nitrile chemistry generates activated nitrile intermediates including acrylonitrile within a broader cyanosulfidic network.
reactions.reasoning: This branch is literature-grounded but less direct than α-ketoglutarate routes. Cyanosulfidic chemistry from HCN/H2S/UV produces nitrile-rich precursor networks, and recent review literature links acrylonitrile chemistry to glutamate/glutamine precursor formation (Patel et al., 2015; Kuroda & Kobayashi, 2024).

reactions.name: Acrylonitrile-to-glutamate aminonitrile precursor conversion

conditions.other: stepwise conjugate addition/aminonitrile-forming chemistry; represented as precursor-pool conversion rather than a fully resolved single mechanism

reactions.mechanism: Activated nitrile chemistry extends the carbon skeleton and installs amino/nitrile functionality to feed the glutamate aminonitrile pool.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is included because the supplied literature explicitly notes that acrylonitrile in cyanosulfidic chemistry affords glutamine or glutamic acid precursors. It is marked as a precursor-pool step rather than a clean isolated reaction, reflecting current mechanistic incompleteness (Kuroda & Kobayashi, 2024; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydroxylamine generation from nitrogen oxides on Fe-sulfur-rich surfaces

conditions.other: indirectly represents reduction of atmospheric NOx to more reduced nitrogen species in surface waters

reactions.mechanism: Atmospherically generated oxidized nitrogen species are reduced on iron-sulfur mineral surfaces to more reduced nitrogen carriers including hydroxylamine/ammonia pools.
reactions.reasoning: This step is added strictly to connect the hydroxylamine-dependent protometabolic glutamate route. The literature supports FeS-mediated reduction of NO to NH3 under mild conditions and identifies nitrogen reduction as an open prebiotic bottleneck; hydroxylamine is therefore included as a justified but partly inferential connector (Summers & Chang, 1993; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Thermal cyclization of glutamate to pyroglutamate

conditions.other: elevated temperature dehydration/cyclization during drydown or prolonged heating

reactions.mechanism: Intramolecular condensation of glutamate forms the five-membered lactam pyroglutamate, decreasing free glutamate accumulation.
reactions.reasoning: This degradation branch is included because glutamate-specific cyclization to pyroglutamate is a key stability constraint highlighted in the literature and helps explain why accumulation can be difficult under warm conditions (Bada et al., 1995; Pérez-Villa et al., 2018).

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolic α-ketoglutarate then transamination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation produces pyruvate; concentrated surface HCN chemistry provides glyoxylate; Fe2+-promoted TCA-like chemistry yields α-ketoglutarate, then alum-catalyzed transamination gives glutamate.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolic α-ketoglutarate then NADH reductive amination
pathways.description: The same α-ketoglutarate hub is converted to glutamate by NADH-mediated reductive amination at room temperature and alkaline pH.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolic α-ketoglutarate then H2/Ni reductive amination
pathways.description: Mild hydrothermal carbon fixation and protometabolism generate α-ketoglutarate, which is aminated by H2 over nickel under compartmentalized conditions.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolic α-ketoglutarate then FeS/H2S reductive amination
pathways.description: α-Ketoglutarate from the hydrothermal protometabolic network is converted to glutamate directly on iron-sulfur minerals.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolic α-ketoglutarate then pyrite photocatalytic amination
pathways.description: After hydrothermal generation of α-ketoglutarate, transfer to UV-exposed pyrite-bearing surface settings enables photocatalytic glutamate formation.

pathways.name: Metal-free α-ketoglutarate then transamination
pathways.description: A metal-free warm aqueous pyruvate-glyoxylate network forms α-ketoglutarate, followed by alum-catalyzed transamination to glutamate.

pathways.name: Metal-free α-ketoglutarate then NADH reductive amination
pathways.description: A second metal-free α-ketoglutarate branch converges on the room-temperature NADH-mediated route to glutamate.

pathways.name: Acrolein Strecker pathway to glutamate
pathways.description: Atmospheric discharge products provide acrolein and HCN, which undergo the classical glutamate-specific Strecker sequence through glutamate aminonitrile and hydrolysis.

pathways.name: HCN oligomerization-hydrolysis background route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN in evaporative or eutectic surface settings oligomerizes and, upon hydrolysis, releases glutamate as a minor product within a complex mixture.

pathways.name: Hydroxylamine-assisted Fe0 protometabolic glutamate route
pathways.description: A network-level route in which hydroxylamine-bearing nitrogen chemistry feeds directly into the Fe0-driven pyruvate-glyoxylate protometabolic system to produce glutamate.
